The plan includes select 8 languages from 4 typological families (Isolating: Chinese, Yoruba; Agglutinative: Japanese, Turkish; Low Fusional: Arabic, Spanish; High Fusional: Hebrew, Ukrainian) and group by resource level (High/Low) based on GPT-3 pre-training data proportions.
The plan includes sample 400 test articles and reference summaries per language from XL-Sum (for 7 languages) and HeSum (for Hebrew).
The plan includes generate system summaries for all 400 samples per language using GPT-3.5-Turbo-0125 and Gemini-1.0-Pro, yielding two summaries per article.

The plan includes corrupt a subset of generated summaries using language-specific strategies: for coherence, replace verbs with lemmas, replace conjunctions, or reorder non-adjacent sentences; for completeness, shuffle named entities with the same label and insert an irrelevant sentence from another article.

The plan includes design the MTurk annotation task: each HIT displays an article, a qualification question, and two system summaries (one from each model); annotators rate each summary on coherence and completeness using a 1–4 Likert scale, with descriptions of each quality criterion.
The plan includes recruit crowd workers via Amazon Mechanical Turk, with 3 annotators per summary pair, and collect ~20,000 total annotations (400 samples × 3 annotators × 2 models per language).
The plan includes aggregate human ratings per summary (e.g., mean of the 3 annotators) to obtain final Likert scores for coherence and completeness.

The plan includes compute n-gram metric scores for all system summaries against gold references: ROUGE-1/2/3/L with default space tokenization, BLEU, CHRF, ROUGE variants with mBERT tokenizer and with language‑specific monolingual tokenizer, and BLEU computed on lemmatized text for fusional languages.

The plan includes compute neural metric scores: MoverScore (with mBERT embeddings), BERTScore (using mBERT and language‑specific BERT models per language), Gemini-as-a-judge (Gemini 1.0 Pro with same prompt as annotators), and COMET (using wmt22‑comet‑da, excluding source input to adapt for summarization).
The plan includes calculate Pearson correlation coefficient between each metric’s scores and the aggregated human Likert ratings, separately for coherence and completeness.

The plan includes group correlations by typological family: Isolating (Chinese, Yoruba), Agglutinative (Japanese, Turkish), Low Fusional (Arabic, Spanish), High Fusional (Hebrew, Ukrainian); compute per‑family averages.

The plan includes group correlations by resource level: High (Chinese, Japanese, Arabic, Spanish) and Low (Yoruba, Turkish, Hebrew, Ukrainian) based on GPT-3 token proportions; compute per‑resource averages.
The plan includes report Pearson r values with statistical significance markers (*p<0.05, **p<0.01).

The plan includes the setting 'full correlation analysis': Evaluation of 21 automatic metrics (n-gram and neural, including tokenizer-adapted variants) using 400 samples per language, 3 annotators per summary, Likert-scale ratings for coherence and completeness, with Pearson correlation and p-value significance testing.
The plan includes the use of the XL-Sum dataset.
The plan includes the use of the HeSum dataset.
The plan includes the use of the GPT-3.5-Turbo-0125 model.
The plan includes the use of the Gemini-1.0-Pro model.

The plan specifies the hyperparameter 'significance_testing' with value p-values reported (*p<0.05, **p<0.01).

The agent has executed the command(s) that produce the results shown in Figure 1 (Elo score trends from human evaluation: Gemini-generated summaries rank higher f...).
The agent has executed the command(s) that produce the results shown in Table 5 (Standard n-gram metrics show negative or weak correlations with human judgments ...).
The agent has executed the command(s) that produce the results shown in Table 6 (COMET yields the highest correlation with human judgments across resource levels...).
The agent has executed the command(s) that produce the results shown in Table 10 (Per-language Pearson correlations for coherence confirm the negative trends in f...).
The agent has executed the command(s) that produce the results shown in Table 11 (Per-language completeness correlations mirror the typological patterns and show ...).

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 1: Elo score trends from human evaluation: Gemini-generated summaries rank higher for high-fusional and low-resource languages, while GPT summaries rank higher for high-resource languages.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 5: Standard n-gram metrics show negative or weak correlations with human judgments for fusional languages (e.g., ROUGE-L -0.23 for high-fusional coherence), while neural metrics like COMET consistently achieve stronger correlations across all typological families (e.g., 0.35 high-fusional coherence).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 6: COMET yields the highest correlation with human judgments across resource levels; monolingual BERTScore outperforms multilingual BERTScore for high-resource languages and low-resource completeness.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 10: Per-language Pearson correlations for coherence confirm the negative trends in fusional languages (Hebrew, Arabic) and the benefits of adapted tokenization/neural metrics.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 11: Per-language completeness correlations mirror the typological patterns and show that lemmatized BLEU improves high-fusional correlations dramatically (e.g., from -0.10 to 0.40).
The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 5): Standard ROUGE-L yields negative Pearson correlation for high-fusional languages: -0.23 for coherence and -0.18 for completeness.

The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 5): Using the mBERT tokenizer with ROUGE-L changes the correlation for high-fusional languages from negative to positive: -0.23 → 0.08 (coherence) and -0.18 → 0.12 (completeness).

The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 5): COMET achieves the highest correlation among all metrics across typological families, with 0.35 for high-fusional coherence and 0.24 for completeness.
The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 5): Applying BLEU on lemmatized text improves correlation for high-fusional completeness from -0.10 to 0.40.

The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 6): Monolingual BERTScore outperforms multilingual BERTScore for high-resource languages (coherence 0.27 vs 0.23) and low-resource completeness (0.21 vs 0.15).
